resources.string: You enabled sounds for this character. Select or create a sounds folder before saving.
The connection is active. Do you really want to go back and close the connection?
You are not connected. Do you want to reconnect?
Message history. Use the arrow keys to read line by line.
Most MUDs use the ISO-8859-1 encoding. If this game does not support UTF-8, accented characters may come out wrong, especially in the commands you send. Use UTF-8 anyway?
Send several commands at once by separating them with this text. The default is space, at sign, space. Leave the field empty to disable.
No voices available. If you just switched engines, close and reopen this dialog.
This is the configured voice of %1$s.
The backup includes characters, triggers, timers and settings.
Importing a backup will replace your current characters, triggers, timers and settings. Do you want to continue?
The backup could not be exported.
The backup could not be imported. Make sure the file is a valid jMud backup.

New version %1$s available.

What's new: %2$s

Do you want to download and install it now?
You are already on the latest version.
The update could not be downloaded. Please try again later.
No saved servers. Register a character first.
No saved characters. Register a character first.

A trigger watches every line that arrives from the game. When a line matches the configured message, the trigger acts on its own: it can send commands, play a sound from the pack, save the line into a named history, or hide the line. Matching ignores upper and lower case.
Match at start: fires when the line begins with the message. Example: the message \"You are hungry\" fires on the line \"You are hungry and weak.\"
Match anywhere: fires when the message appears anywhere in the line. Example: the message \"arrives from\" fires on \"A merchant arrives from the north.\"
Match at end: fires when the line ends with the message. Example: the message \"shouts at you!\" fires on \"The guard shouts at you!\"
Whole line: fires only when the entire line is exactly equal to the message.
Wildcard pattern: the message describes the whole line using symbols that match the parts that vary. It is the most powerful type; see the wildcards below.

In the wildcard pattern type, the asterisk (*) matches any stretch of text, the at sign (@) matches one word, the ampersand (&) matches a number, and the question mark (?) matches a single character. Each wildcard captures what it matched and becomes $1, $2, $3 and so on, in the order it appears, for use in the commands. The pattern must describe the entire line, from start to end. To search for one of these symbols literally, write a backslash before it, such as \\*.

In the commands, $0 is the whole line and $1 to $9 are the pieces captured by the pattern wildcards. There are also $s, $t, $u and so on up to $y: counting the words from the point in the line where the match happened, $s is the second word, $t is the third, and so on. $z inserts a line break, separating two commands. Each line of the commands field is sent as a separate command.
Eat when hungry. Message: \"You are hungry\", match at start. Commands, one per line: \"get bread bag\" and \"eat bread\".

Look at whoever arrives. Message: \"* arrives from the *.\", wildcard pattern. On the line \"A merchant arrives from the north.\", $1 becomes \"A merchant\" and $2 becomes \"north\". Command: \"look $1\".
Store received coins. Message: \"You receive & coin*\", wildcard pattern. On the line \"You receive 50 gold coins.\", $1 becomes \"50\". Command: \"deposit $1 coins\".
Danger sound alert. Message: \"shouts at you!\", match at end, with the sound name field set to a sound from the pack, for example \"alert\". The sound plays whenever someone shouts at you.
Silence repetitive messages. Message: \"The rain keeps falling\", match at start, with the ignore in history option enabled. The line is neither shown in the history nor spoken.
There is nothing left to ignore.
No command was recorded. Recording discarded.

Macros are command shortcuts you create. There are two ways to create one.

Manual command: on the Macros tab, set a name and a list of commands, one per line. To run it, type # followed by the name during the game, for example #tapa. If you type anything else after the name, that text is automatically appended to the end of the last command. For example, a macro named "tt" with the command "lan telep", run as #tt zanand, sends "lan telep zanand", with nothing special to write in the command.

For more control, this is optional: using $1, $2 and so on inside the commands, each one receives a word typed after the name, and $0 receives the whole typed text at once, which lets you place the typed text in the middle of a command or repeat it across more than one line. Once the commands already use $1, $2 or $0, the text is no longer appended automatically; it only goes where you placed it.

Recording: through the More options button on the game screen, under Record macro, you record a sequence of commands while playing, useful for mapping an area. Start recording begins capturing every command you send and takes you straight back to the game screen, ready to type. Pause recording stops capturing without losing what was already recorded, and the same button turns into Resume recording to continue later. Ignore last command removes the most recent recorded command, one at a time; once there is nothing left to remove, the app lets you know. Stop recording ends the capture: if any command was recorded, a screen opens to name and save the macro, with the option to copy all commands to the clipboard before saving.

Like triggers and timers, each macro has a scope: all games, one server or a specific character. A disabled macro does not respond when its name is typed.
Do you want to open this link in the browser?
The sound pack could not be installed.
The sound pack could not be installed. Reason: %1$s
This character doesn\'t have a sounds folder yet. Select or create one before continuing.
Storage permission is required to download or import sound packs. Grant the permission and try again.
Warning: Connection closed by the server.
Warning: The command could not be sent.
Delete all saved logs? This action cannot be undone.
Sound folders live in the device\'s internal storage, inside the Documents folder, in the jMud folder. Here you can create and delete folders and assign a folder to a character.
No sound folders found. Create a folder here or install a sound pack through the More options button on the game screen.
Delete the folder %1$s and all files inside it? This action cannot be undone.
Warning: the characters %1$s use this folder and will be left without a sounds folder.
No characters saved. Create a character on the Characters tab first.
Open the project page on GitHub

Great to have you here! jMud has a complete help guide that presents every feature of the app, page by page: characters, the game screen, triggers, timers, sounds and much more. Would you like to explore the features now? You can also open this presentation at any time in Settings, in the About this app section.

jMud is a MUD client built to be fully accessible with screen readers. MUDs are multiplayer text games played over the internet: the server sends descriptions and events as text, and you play by typing commands.

The home screen has four tabs at the bottom: Characters, to save your games and connect; Triggers, to react automatically to game messages; Timers, to repeat commands at intervals; and Settings, to adjust speech, sounds, logs and backup.

While playing, the same tabs appear at the top of the screen, so you can create triggers, timers and change settings without disconnecting.

Use the Previous and Next buttons to move through this help. You can reopen it at any time in Settings, in the About this app section.

On the Characters tab you save the games you play most. Tap Add and enter the character name, the server address, the port and, if you want, the password.

With automatic login enabled, jMud sends the name and password as soon as the connection is established, and you enter the game without typing anything. In the post-connection commands field you can list commands, one per line, to be sent right after login.

Each character also stores its own speech and sound preferences, plus the sounds folder used by that game. With the Choose Folder button you select an existing folder or create a new one, and the app suggests a name based on the server address. Where the folders live and how to manage them is explained in detail on the Sounds and sound packs page of this help.

To connect, just tap the character in the list. A long press opens the edit and remove options; these actions are also available in the screen reader actions menu.

The Manual Connection button lets you connect to any server by entering just the address and port, without saving a character.

The game screen shows the message history, the command field and the Send button.

At the top there are two switches: Speech, which controls whether incoming messages are spoken, and Sounds, which controls sound effects. The More options button gathers other actions: enabling or disabling triggers and timers, downloading or importing sound packs, and disconnecting.

In the history, you can read the messages line by line with your screen reader. The Scroll to end button jumps to the most recent messages, and Clear history clears the screen.

Type in the command field and tap Send. With the field empty, Send repeats the last command, which is handy for walking or repeating actions.

If you use a physical keyboard, the Playing with the keyboard page of this help explains the available shortcuts.

A long press on the Send button opens the named histories, separate lists that triggers can feed, such as a chat channel or combat records. When a message contains a link, you can open it by tapping the line.

If you connect a physical keyboard to your device, jMud offers shortcuts to play quickly.

With focus on the command field: the Enter key sends what is written and, with the field empty, sends the last command again, which makes it easy to repeat actions and walk around the game. The Page Up and Page Down keys read the history line by line without taking focus away from the field: the review starts at the most recent message, Page Up goes back to earlier lines and Page Down moves forward to newer ones. Each line is announced by the screen reader. When you send a command, the review returns to the end of the history.

With focus on the history list: the up and down arrows read line by line, Page Up and Page Down jump ten lines at a time, Home goes to the first message, End goes to the last one and Escape returns focus to the command field.

Note: keyboard usage in the app may vary depending on the keyboard and the screen reader in use, which can behave differently from each other.

You can send several commands at once by separating them with space, at sign, space. For example: say good morning! @ say how are you? sends both commands in sequence, in the order they were written.

The at sign was chosen because it is easy to reach on a phone keyboard, but the separator is yours: in Settings, in the Commands section, you can change it to a semicolon or any other text, or leave the field empty to disable the feature.

Because the default separator includes the surrounding spaces, an at sign attached to another word, as in an e-mail address, does not split anything.

Triggers are automatic reactions to game messages. When an incoming line matches the configured text, jMud can send commands in response, play a sound from your sounds folder, store the line in a named history, or ignore the line, silencing and hiding repetitive messages.

Each trigger has a scope: it can apply to all games, to a specific server or to a single character. You can also disable a trigger without deleting it, or disable all of them at once through the More options button on the game screen.

The match types include starts with, contains, ends with, exact message and pattern with variables, which lets you capture parts of the message and use them in the response commands.

The Triggers tab has its own Help button with a detailed explanation of each type, the patterns and the variables, with several ready-made examples.

Timers send commands automatically at intervals while you are connected. When creating one, you set the interval in seconds and the commands, one per line.

They are useful to keep the connection alive in games that disconnect idle players, to repeat training actions, or for any routine that needs to happen regularly.

Like triggers, each timer has a scope: all games, one server or a specific character. You can disable a timer individually by editing it, or disable all of them through the More options button on the game screen.

Timers only run while the connection is open; when you disconnect, they all stop automatically.

jMud supports the MSP protocol, used by MUDs to play sound effects and music. When the game supports it and the Sounds switch is on, sounds play automatically during the session.

Where the folders live: in the device's internal storage there is a folder called Documents, and inside it the jMud folder. Each subfolder of the jMud folder is a sound folder. In a file manager, the path is Internal storage, then Documents, then jMud. Note: it is the Documents folder of the internal storage, not the Documents shortcut shown on the home screen of some file managers.

How the client uses the folders: each character has an assigned sound folder, chosen when creating or editing the character, which keeps each game's sounds separate. When the game or a trigger asks for a sound by name, jMud looks for the file inside that character's folder, automatically completing the wav, mp3 and ogg extensions. Sounds the server sends by internet address are downloaded once and kept in a cache.

To install sounds, use the More options button on the game screen: Download sound pack downloads and installs a pack from a link, and Import ZIP pack installs a file already on your device. Many sound-enabled MUDs distribute these packs on their websites.

To keep everything organized, the Manage sounds section in Settings lists your folders with the number of files in each one, shows which characters use each folder, and lets you create a folder, delete a folder and assign a folder to a character without editing the character.

Triggers can also play a sound by file name, which lets you create your own sound alerts even in games without MSP.

jMud can record everything that happens in each game session: the messages received and the commands sent. Logs are organized by session, with date and time, character and server.

In the Logs section of Settings you turn recording on or off, choose how many days the files are kept, from seven days to never delete, and can delete them all at once.

Logs are useful to reread a scene, review a fight or keep important conversations.

In the Backup section of Settings you can export all your data to a single file with the jmud extension: characters, triggers, timers, macros and preferences.

Keep this file somewhere safe, such as your favorite cloud service. When switching devices or reinstalling the app, use Import backup to restore everything at once.

Warning: importing replaces your current data with the data from the file. Sound files are not included in the backup; if needed, reinstall the sound packs through the More options button on the game screen.

The About this app section, in Settings, shows the installed version and gathers the Check for update, Open the project page on GitHub and Help buttons, the last one reopening this presentation.

jMud notifies you automatically when a new version is available: a window appears with the news and the option to download and install it right away.

This app is free and open source, created especially for blind players. Suggestions and problem reports are very welcome on the project page on GitHub or through the Send a suggestion by e-mail button, which writes to augustoanacletoprojetos@gmail.com.

Good adventure, and happy gaming!
